title: Contract expressions with side effects produce debug/release behavioral divergence
description: The spec says contract expressions 'should be pure' but 'the compiler does not enforce this in v1'. A requires or ensures clause that allocates, writes state, or has I/O will run in debug mode and be stripped in release. The program has different observable behavior in the two modes. This is not a Heisenbug risk — it is a guaranteed behavioral divergence. Zig's entire value proposition is no hidden control flow. What caller observes: a test suite that passes in ReleaseSafe but fails in Debug, or vice versa, with no compiler feedback explaining why.
design: Emit a compiler warning for any contract expression that calls a function with a void return type or that takes a mutable pointer argument (*T, not *const T). These are reliable surface indicators of side effects. Does not require a 'pure' concept in the language. Warning fires at the contract declaration site, not the call site. Can be suppressed with a per-contract annotation if the caller has verified the expression is safe. Goal: create friction at the right point without blocking v1.
